summary: [insight] Stepan Company is the sole customer in this Chemicals peer group, which limits direct peer comparison but reveals an industry-critical architectural failure: the integration and data foundation (BTP Integration Suite down 17%, Business Data Cloud at $0, CPEA at $0) that specialty chemicals companies require for connected operations is completely dormant. In an industry where procurement spend optimization, regulatory compliance, and workforce safety are tightly coupled, Stepan's fragmented point-tool architecture — with Hire-to-Retire and Source-to-Pay chains operating as disconnected islands — prevents the integrated chemical manufacturing operating model that justifies enterprise SAP investment and exposes the company to compliance and cost-control gaps that competitors with active integration fabrics would not face.

[insight] Stepan exhibits a 'shelfware at the foundation, thriving at the edges' pattern that is particularly damaging for chemicals companies: analytics and reporting modules (Concur analytics >110%) run strong while transactional cores collapse (Concur Expense 82% May decline), and strategic platform components (BDC $4,807 contracted/$0 consumed, Catalog at 0.5%) sit idle. For a specialty chemicals manufacturer where raw material price volatility and supplier risk require tight Source-to-Pay integration, this inverted consumption profile means Stepan can report on spend but cannot control it — a competitive disadvantage against chemicals peers who have activated their procurement platforms end-to-end.

[insight] Stepan's dormant Preferred Success entitlements ($57,705 HCM unused, $9,765/month Ariba passive) represent the largest immediate consumption recovery opportunity in this portfolio. In the chemicals industry where EHS-trained workforce availability and supplier qualification directly impact plant uptime and regulatory standing, these unused advisory services could be redirected toward activating the integration and AI layers (Joule Studio flat at $580/month) that would connect talent pipelines to operational readiness and procurement workflows to compliance — capabilities that leading chemicals companies treat as operational necessities, not optional enhancements.

[insight] The complete absence of AI and advanced analytics foundation activation (Joule Studio flat, Business Data Cloud $0, CPEA $0) places Stepan behind the trajectory that chemicals industry leaders are pursuing. As the sector moves toward predictive demand planning, automated regulatory reporting, and AI-assisted formulation management, Stepan's dormant AI substrate means the company cannot participate in these capability shifts regardless of application-layer adoption — a strategic gap that will widen with each quarter of platform inactivity.

[action] Convene a Chemicals Operating Model Architecture Review in June positioning BTP Integration Suite, Business Data Cloud, and CPEA as the shared substrate for specialty chemicals operations — not as three separate technology contracts — with explicit focus on connecting Hire-to-Retire (workforce safety, EHS certification tracking) and Source-to-Pay (raw material procurement, supplier compliance) into the integrated chemical manufacturing process chains that drive regulatory compliance and cost control; this single intervention addresses the root cause behind all four L1 domain consumption gaps.

[action] Redirect the $67,470+ in dormant Preferred Success entitlements (HCM and Ariba combined) into a 45-day Joule-anchored integration workshop that forces activation across the dormant platform layers while simultaneously addressing the declining talent and procurement modules; for a chemicals company, prioritize use cases around supplier risk scoring, EHS workforce certification workflows, and raw material spend analytics — capabilities that translate platform activation into measurable operational outcomes.

[action] Establish a monthly Cross-Domain Platform Health checkpoint with explicit chemicals-industry run-rate gates: CPEA activation by July for regulatory analytics, Integration Suite restored to $4,250+/month to support batch traceability and supplier connectivity, Catalog above 25% by Q3 to enable raw material standardization, and Concur Expense submitter-base restored to ensure T&E compliance across plant operations — tracking these as one architectural program rather than four LoB scorecards to protect the $144,897 ACV renewal credibility ahead of FY27.

[insight] Kraft Heinz leads this Consumer Products cohort on transactional execution breadth — Ariba Buying at 111%, Business Network transmitting POs, PaPM bursting to ceiling, and Joule Studio scaling — but is simultaneously the most architecturally fragile because its integration fabric (Integration Suite eroding from 134% to 80% attainment, BTP Database Cloud at 0%, SAC BI at $0 consumed) is collapsing beneath these workloads. Domino's and S.C. Johnson show healthier integration plumbing (Integration Suite at 99% and declining but still functional respectively), yet neither has activated the intelligence layers above. The net effect: Kraft Heinz is running the most SAP workloads but on the weakest foundation, creating the highest near-term disruption risk in the peer group.

[insight] S.C. Johnson exhibits the most severe 'inverted pyramid' architecture in this cohort — value layers like BDC analytics (4-5x ceiling) and Concur ExpenseIt are over-consuming while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement foundation sits at effectively 0% across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance. Neither Domino's (HANA Cloud at 75%, SAP Build at 53%) nor Kraft Heinz (BTP Public at 1326% but Database Cloud at 0%) shows this complete foundation void. For a CPG company dependent on rapid reformulation and supplier resiliency, S.C. Johnson's missing BTP runtime means every cross-domain integration — PLM-to-manufacturing, Concur-to-Blackline-to-S/4, BDC persistence — runs on manual workarounds that will fail at scale or during the next S/4 upgrade cycle.

[insight] All three Consumer Products customers share an identical architectural anti-pattern: transactional/execution layers are live and scaling while governance, intelligence, and supplier collaboration layers are dormant or declining — Domino's has Ariba Buying at 99.96% but Sourcing/Contracts at 66% and Business Network flat; Kraft Heinz runs Ariba Buying at 111% but Sourcing at 35%, Spend Analysis dark, and Supplier Risk at $0; S.C. Johnson has Business Network frozen at $48.6K monthly with IPD at 5.7%. For an industry facing commodity inflation, ESG traceability mandates, and private-label competition, this pattern means none of these enterprises can execute the spend governance, supplier risk visibility, or forecast collaboration that defends CPG margin — they are paying for connected value chains but operating as disconnected transaction processors.

[insight] Domino's is the only customer in this cohort with a contractually coherent BTP-anchored clean-core strategy (Build + Integration Suite + Custom Domain + HANA Cloud), but execution has stalled — SAP Build collapsed to 52.7%, Custom Domain is flat, and no net-new franchisee or loyalty apps have launched. Kraft Heinz and S.C. Johnson never contracted this extensibility vision at comparable depth. This positions Domino's as the peer group's best candidate for rapid BTP activation because the commercial and architectural foundation exists; the gap is purely operational activation, whereas Kraft Heinz and S.C. Johnson would require contract restructuring before similar workloads could land.

[action] Launch a coordinated 'Governance Layer Activation' initiative across all three customers within 60 days, targeting the shared dormant intelligence and control products: Ariba Sourcing/Contracts and Supplier Risk reactivation on 3-5 anchor spend categories at each company, Business Network forecast collaboration tied to IBP or demand planning cycles, and Spend Analysis dashboards sourced from existing Buying transaction data. Frame this as a CPG margin-defense program — supplier risk visibility, sourcing compliance, and demand-supply synchronization are table-stakes capabilities that all three have contracted but none has operationalized, and the VAT can run a single enablement motion with industry-specific use cases rather than three isolated product conversations.

[action] Prioritize Kraft Heinz for an emergency Integration Suite and BTP foundation recovery sprint before end of Q2 — the combination of eroding integration fabric and accelerating consumption layers creates the highest near-term disruption risk in the portfolio. Convene a joint architecture session with their CIO and Chief Supply Chain Officer within 30 days to mandate Integration Suite as the backbone for all Joule, Ariba, Business Network, and Signavio flows, and activate BTP Database Cloud and SAC BI on one anchor use case (finance dashboard or supply chain KPI layer) to arrest the regression before the $3.5M+ Business Network investment and scaling AI agents become stranded on point-to-point plumbing.

[action] Use Domino's as the BTP activation proof point for the cohort — stand up a 90-day 'BTP Activation Squad' to land 3-5 net-new workloads (franchisee portal, supplier onboarding handoff, store-level demand sensing) that consume the already-contracted HANA Cloud, SAP Build, Integration Suite, and Custom Domain stack end-to-end. Success at Domino's creates a replicable CPG playbook the VAT can adapt for Kraft Heinz's Joule and Signavio extensibility needs and S.C. Johnson's eventual CPEA activation, converting one customer's stalled clean-core ambition into an industry-wide consumption catalyst.

[action] Initiate a unified FY26 contract rebaselining conversation across the cohort before Q3 close, treating over-consumption and under-consumption as a single commercial event at each customer: true-up Domino's Concur T&E (breach risk by October) and Kraft Heinz's PaPM/BTP Public (already at ceiling) while converting dormant ACV — an estimated $200K+ at Domino's, $2M+ at Kraft Heinz, and $500K+ at S.C. Johnson — into contractually-linked activation commitments tied to the governance and foundation roadmaps above. This repositions FY27 renewals from fragmented downsizing negotiations into enterprise renewal conversations anchored to demonstrated business value.

[insight] Abbott Laboratories leads Eli Lilly on integration infrastructure maturity — Abbott's Integration Suite runs at 107-112% of plan with active middleware flows, while Lilly's Integration Suite is flatlined at ~$25K/month with $24.6K YTD unused and BDC Public collapsed 79%. However, this leadership is hollow: Abbott's integration output terminates nowhere downstream, meaning both companies share the same architectural failure mode — integration fabric disconnected from business process closure — but Abbott has at least built the pipes while Lilly has not yet turned them on.

[insight] Eli Lilly is ahead of Abbott on AI and process intelligence adoption — Joule Studio at 171% and Signavio PM expanded 2.5x demonstrate Lilly is actively investing in the agent and process mining surfaces that define next-generation pharma operations, while Abbott shows no comparable AI or process intelligence consumption signals. For life sciences companies facing pressure to accelerate drug development cycles and manufacturing efficiency, Lilly's bet on process intelligence positions them ahead of Abbott on operational transformation — but without the integration substrate to connect these investments, Lilly risks building intelligent islands rather than an intelligent enterprise.

[insight] Both Abbott and Eli Lilly exhibit an identical and industry-critical architectural anti-pattern: fully consumed enablement and success wrappers sitting atop dormant platform workloads. Abbott over-consumes Integration Suite, SIM, and Preferred Success Ariba while SAC, HANA Cloud, Build, and Ariba Buying lag; Lilly pins all Preferred Success entitlements at 100%+ while HXM Operations (38%), Commerce Cloud (45%), SAC Planning (4.5%), and DRC (0.17%) remain shelfware. In life sciences, where regulatory scrutiny demands demonstrable value from technology investments, both companies are paying premium prices for guided adoption that is not converting into production GxP workloads — exposing a combined ~$700K+ of ACV to renewal risk across the cohort.

[insight] Eli Lilly is significantly behind Abbott on pharma-critical GxP and regulated execution readiness — Lilly's Batch Release Hub sits at $0 consumption against $32K/month ACV, Document & Reporting Compliance is at 0.17% across 90+ countries, and IBP Supply/Inventory/Response modules are dormant, leaving the digital thread from demand signal through batch release to statutory reporting broken at three of four nodes. Abbott's supply chain anchor (~$1.48M annual) and IBP investment at least show ratable consumption, even if under-activated. For pharmaceutical manufacturers where electronic batch disposition and e-invoicing/VAT controls are audit-critical, Lilly's dormant GxP stack represents a material compliance and commercial exposure that Abbott has partially mitigated.

[action] Execute a coordinated 'Closed-Loop Architecture Review' across both Abbott and Eli Lilly within Q2 FY2026 — position this as an industry-standard intervention for life sciences companies where regulatory and operational integrity depends on end-to-end process closure (source-to-pay, plan-to-deliver, demand-to-batch-release, record-to-report). Use Abbott's over-consumed Integration Suite as the positive proof point for Lilly's dormant integration fabric, and use Lilly's Joule/Signavio investments as the aspirational target for Abbott's next-phase AI activation — creating peer pressure and cross-customer learning that accelerates consumption at both accounts.

[action] Launch an industry-specific 'Preferred Success Re-Pointing' initiative across the cohort within 60 days that contractually redirects all fully-consumed success entitlements at both Abbott and Lilly to named, under-adopted platform workloads aligned to GxP and regulated-execution priorities — specifically targeting Batch Release Hub, DRC statutory reporting, IBP module activation, and SAC Planning go-live. Frame this to both CIOs as an industry-standard practice for pharma companies to ensure success spend produces audit-defensible platform adoption rather than generic advisory ahead of FY27 renewals.

[action] Escalate a joint VAT briefing to SAP industry leadership on the systemic 'enablers over outcomes' pattern observed across both Abbott and Lilly — the cohort has contracted for $2.2M ACV but is burning integration, success, and incentive capacity while leaving planning, procurement execution, AI substrate, and GxP automation dormant. Recommend a life sciences practice playbook that sequences platform adoption to match regulatory and operational value chains, preventing future customers from replicating this architectural debt and giving the VAT a repeatable framework to protect the $9.4M budget across the cohort.

[insight] Marathon Petroleum is operating as a fragmented SAP environment rather than an integrated digital core — the refining sector's margin optimization imperative demands real-time signal integration across spend, operations, and financial data, yet Marathon's BTP integration backbone (BDC Public at 0%, Integration Suite dormant, Finance BTP at zero consumption) leaves S/4HANA, Ariba, Signavio, SAC Planning, and Joule functioning as five disconnected product islands, directly blocking the closed-loop visibility that differentiates high-performing downstream operators from laggards in volatile crack-spread environments.

[insight] Marathon exhibits a systemic 'execution without governance' anti-pattern that creates material risk for refining operations — Database Cloud at 537% consumption versus BDC at 0%, Joule Studio at 320% versus dormant Integration Suite, and Ariba Buying at 138% versus Catalog at 35% means the company is industrializing transactional throughput while starving the control layers that enforce compliance, spend discipline, and architectural coherence, a posture that in the highly regulated downstream energy sector accelerates off-contract spend, audit exposure, and margin leakage simultaneously.

[insight] Marathon's commercial envelope is materially misaligned with actual consumption architecture — projected overages of ~$108K on Database Cloud, ~$155K on Ariba Buying, and 3.2x plan on Joule Studio will collide with 35-100% stranded entitlements on BDC Public, BTP Foundation, Finance BTP, Ariba Catalog, and Preferred Success, meaning Marathon will exit FY2026 having overpaid for runtime while underutilizing the data product, integration, and advisory capabilities that convert SAP investments into the margin discipline and operational agility refining economics demand.

[insight] As a single-customer cohort, Marathon Petroleum represents both the capability leader and the laggard within this oil and gas portfolio — leading on transactional platform adoption (S/4HANA Private Cloud, Ariba P2P, SAC Planning, Joule all actively consuming) but lagging critically on the integration and data fabric layer that downstream energy operators require to correlate crude acquisition costs, refining throughput, and product margin in real time; without peer benchmarks in this portfolio, Marathon's own contracted-but-dormant BTP/BDC architecture serves as the internal baseline it is failing to meet.

[action] Within 30 days, convene a Chief Architect-level Enterprise Integration and Data Fabric review with Marathon that treats BTP, Integration Suite, and BDC as one unified backbone for refining margin optimization — map each over-consuming workload (Joule agents for operational intelligence, Ariba Buying for feedstock procurement, Database Cloud for transactional scale, Signavio findings for process efficiency, SAC Planning models for margin forecasting) to the specific APIs, events, and governed data products each requires, and commit a sequenced June-September activation plan with named business owners tied to Marathon's H2 planning cycle.

[action] Execute a consolidated FY2026 commercial true-up and FY2027 baseline reset within 45 days that addresses Database Cloud, Ariba Buying, and Joule Studio overages as a single package, funded by repurposing structurally underutilized entitlements (BDC Public, BTP Foundation Other, Finance BTP, Ariba Catalog headroom, Preferred Success) — this prevents mid-year service constraints while re-anchoring contract shape to Marathon's true consumption architecture and strengthening the renewal narrative around business outcomes rather than infrastructure overage management.

[action] Launch a 'governance and control layer' rescue program within 14 days as a single CFO/COO-sponsored executive conversation covering three starved control points — Ariba Catalog re-enablement to enforce spend compliance on feedstock and MRO procurement, Preferred Success sponsor re-alignment to provide S/4HANA Private Cloud architectural guidance, and Signavio Process Management CoE cadence linked to Process Intelligence findings for continuous refining process optimization — framing the intervention as margin discipline infrastructure essential to downstream energy competitiveness rather than isolated technology gaps.

[insight] Border States Industries is the sole representative in this wholesale distribution cohort, making it the de facto benchmark — but measured against industry-standard architectural maturity, the company is operating as five disconnected point solutions rather than one integrated wholesale distribution platform, which is a critical deficiency given that wholesale distributors live and die by inventory velocity, margin protection, and customer service speed that only cross-domain data flows can enable.

[insight] Border States shows an inverted consumption pattern that is architecturally unhealthy for wholesale distribution: the Technology Foundation layer (BTP, Integration Suite, Joule) is running hot at 119-209% while the business outcome layers — Data & AI at 62% unallocated, Customer Experience with a $183K ACV gap, HCM Compensation at 0% — remain cold. In wholesale distribution, where same-day fulfillment and dynamic pricing require real-time data orchestration, burning platform capacity without wiring it to Commerce, Finance, or Analytics means Border States is paying for integration horsepower that never reaches the warehouse floor or the customer portal.

[insight] The absence of a BDC-to-Datasphere/SAC data pipeline is a wholesale distribution-specific risk for Border States — distributors competing against Amazon Business and Grainger require demand sensing, inventory optimization, and margin analytics that only a functioning data fabric can deliver. Border States has the Business Data Cloud infrastructure but no consumption surface, leaving pricing decisions, inventory positioning, and customer segmentation as manual or legacy-system-dependent processes while $215K of Data & AI budget sits dormant.

[insight] Border States' Commerce Cloud implementation without Service, Marketing, or CDP integration creates a broken customer experience loop that wholesale distribution competitors have already closed — B2B buyers expect account-specific pricing, order history visibility, and proactive service alerts as table stakes. Running Commerce as a standalone storefront rather than an orchestrated customer engagement platform puts Border States at a competitive disadvantage against digitally mature distributors who treat every customer touchpoint as a data capture and service opportunity.

[action] Execute an Enterprise Architecture Review in Q3 with Border States' executive leadership (CIO, CFO, CHRO, Head of Digital Commerce) to map the four critical integration gaps — Performance-to-Compensation, BDC-to-Datasphere/SAC, Commerce-to-Service/Marketing, and Concur Expense-to-Pay/Audit — onto a single Integration Suite roadmap that converts existing BTP overconsumption into the connective tissue wholesale distribution operations require for inventory visibility, margin optimization, and unified customer experience.

[action] Initiate a FY2026 contract rebalancing conversation within 45 days that ties the required Technology Foundation and Concur true-ups (~$17K+) to explicit activation commitments on dormant ACV — SuccessFactors Compensation for the next merit cycle, one net-new Commerce Cloud workload integrating Service or Marketing, and one Data & AI use case on BDC for demand sensing or margin analytics — ensuring commercial expansion funds outcome delivery rather than point-solution accumulation.

[action] Launch a 90-day wholesale distribution-specific use case sprint focused on three high-impact integration flows: (1) real-time inventory and pricing data from BDC into Datasphere for demand-responsive stocking, (2) Commerce Cloud to Service Cloud integration for order-to-issue resolution visibility, and (3) unified spend analytics connecting Concur to SAC for procurement cost optimization — each flow directly addresses margin protection and customer retention priorities that define wholesale distribution competitiveness.

enterprise_architecture_insights: [insight] Abbott's enterprise SAP footprint shows a consistent 'pipes contracted, processes dormant' pattern across every L1 domain: Integration Suite runs at 107-112% while SAP Build sits at 41% of plan; HANA Cloud is 35-40% under-utilized while SAC BI is throttled; Ariba Sourcing executes but Buying & Invoicing and Supplier Risk lag at 56% and 21%; C4C plateaus at ~60% while SIM pays out $52K/month; IBP burns ratably with no evidence of multi-module activation. Architecturally, Abbott has bought an integrated suite but is operating it as a federation of point tools — which means the cross-domain value chains (lead-to-cash, source-to-pay, plan-to-deliver, record-to-report) that justify SAP's premium over best-of-breed are not being realized, putting an estimated $400K+ of FY2026 ACV at structural renewal risk across domains.

[insight] The common dependency under every domain gap is the same: integration flows from Integration Suite are not landing in HANA Cloud persistence, are not feeding SAC for planning and BI, are not powering C4C-to-SIM crediting, and are not connecting Ariba Sourcing outcomes into Buying and Supplier Risk. Integration Suite is the only platform component running above plan, but its output is being consumed almost nowhere downstream — meaning Abbott's foundational middleware is succeeding technically while failing architecturally, because the business-process closures (closed-loop planning, closed-loop incentive, closed-loop procurement, closed-loop S&OP) it was bought to enable remain open.

[insight] Holistic contract posture shows Abbott has committed materially across five L1 areas (Supply Chain ~$1.48M annual / $618K YTD as the anchor, Finance & Spend with ~$337K SAC Planning + Ariba stack, BAIP Data & AI ~$107K at risk, BAIP Tech Foundation with the Build $3.5K gap, and CX with $13.8K/month C4C against $52K/month SIM) — but the value-realization gradient is inverted: the largest dollar commitments (IBP, SAC Planning, HANA Cloud) are the most architecturally dormant, while the smallest commitments (Integration Suite, SIM, Preferred Success Ariba) are saturated. Abbott is over-consuming the enablers and under-consuming the outcomes, which is the precise pattern that produces flat renewals and blocks expansion at FY2027.

[action] Convene a single Enterprise Architecture Review with Abbott in Q2 FY2026 that maps the closed-loop business processes (source-to-pay, lead-to-cash-to-incentive, integrated business planning, plan-to-analyze) end-to-end across all L1 domains and identifies the 5-7 specific integration flows from Integration Suite that must terminate in HANA Cloud, SAC, SIM, Ariba Buying, and IBP to close each loop — replacing the current product-by-product true-up conversations with one architectural roadmap tied to documented business outcomes ahead of FY2027 renewal.

[action] Reposition the FY2026 commercial conversation as a single enterprise renewal architecture rather than 12+ product true-ups: bundle the SAC BI expansion, HANA Cloud workload migration, Build activation, C4C capability activation, Ariba Buying/Supplier Risk lift, and IBP module activation under one executive sponsor at Abbott, using the over-consumed assets (Integration Suite, SIM, Preferred Success Ariba) as proof points that capacity expansion drives outcomes — protecting the ~$400K of at-risk ACV across domains in one motion.

[action] Stand up an enterprise-level adoption scorecard within 60 days that measures cross-domain process completion (S&OP cycles closed, sourced suppliers transacting in Buying, CRM-sourced deals crediting in SIM, SAC dashboards persisted on HANA Cloud, Build apps deployed on Integration Suite APIs) rather than per-product dollar attainment — giving Abbott's CIO and SAP joint visibility into architectural value realization and creating the evidence base for FY2027 expansion rather than flat renewal.

solutions_l1.name: BAIP - Data & AI
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Abbott's BAIP Data & AI stack is architecturally lopsided: the analytics consumption layer (SAC BI) is throttled at its ACV ceiling with $933 of Q1 latent demand spilling over, while the underlying data foundation (HANA Cloud) sits 35-40% under-utilized at ~$8.8K/month against a $13.8K budget. This is a classic 'consumption-tier saturated, foundation-tier dormant' pattern — Abbott is extracting visualization value from SAC but has not landed the mission-critical workloads (analytics marts, Datasphere integrations, app persistence) that HANA Cloud is architected to anchor, leaving $107K of FY2026 ACV at risk of erosion.

[insight] The data integration spine (Data Intelligence Cloud) is the only product running to plan at 99.1% YTD attainment, but with 58% of annual ACV ($17,696) unscheduled for Jun-Dec, there is no orchestration roadmap visible in the back half. Architecturally this matters because DIC is the connective tissue that should be feeding HANA Cloud workloads and SAC BI datasets — without a loaded Jun-Dec pipeline, the under-utilization of HANA Cloud is unlikely to self-correct, since net-new integration use cases are what would drive persistence and analytics demand downstream.

[action] Run a single combined workshop within 30 days that maps Abbott's SAC BI overage use cases backward to HANA Cloud persistence and DIC integration flows — use the $933 Q1 SAC overage as proof of latent analytics demand to justify migrating 2-3 workloads onto HANA Cloud, targeting a step-up from $8.8K to $13.8K+/month and closing the structural foundation gap before Q3.

[action] Open a coordinated commercial conversation with Abbott covering all three products simultaneously: expand SAC BI ACV to cover the $13,860 Jun-Dec budget gap and prevent throttling, load DIC ACV across Jun-Dec to give the $17,696 remaining value a consumption ceiling to pace against, and commit HANA Cloud to a monthly attainment plan above 90% — treating these as one architectural renewal rather than three isolated true-ups.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption is tracking just below plan but well within healthy range — 5 months in, Abbott has consumed $9,049 against a budget of $9,128 (99.1% attainment YTD), with only Feb (84.3%) as a meaningful dip. This is a stable, well-adopted workload, not a turnaround case — the risk is complacency, not abandonment.

[insight] Total FY2026 ACV is $30,336 but only $12,640 is contracted across Jan–May with $0 ACV loaded for Jun–Dec — meaning $17,696 (58% of annual value) sits in unscheduled months. Without ACV allocation in the back half, there is no consumption ceiling to pace against, and the customer could either underconsume the remaining budget of $13,068 or hit a true-up surprise.

[action] Schedule a mid-year adoption review with Abbott's Data Intelligence Cloud owner to validate that the Feb dip (-15.7 pts vs plan) was a one-off and confirm the pipeline of data integration/orchestration use cases that will sustain the ~$1,850/month run rate through December.

[action] Work with the SAP commercial team to clarify the Jun–Dec ACV loading — if the $17,696 remaining annual value is intended to be consumed, build a month-by-month consumption plan with Abbott now so the customer doesn't enter Q4 with $13,068 of unconsumed budget.

[insight] Consumption ran 12.5% over budget for three consecutive months (Jan-Mar at $2,796 vs $2,486 budget) before snapping exactly to ACV ceiling in Apr-May at $2,486 — this pattern signals the customer hit their contract cap and is now being throttled, not that demand cooled. Cumulative overage of $933 across Q1 indicates latent demand that exceeds the current contract envelope.

[insight] ACV is contracted only through May ($12,430 total) while budget extends through December ($26,290 total), leaving a $13,860 budget gap across Jun-Dec with no ACV coverage. The customer is actively consuming at 100%+ of available ACV but has no contractual runway for the remaining 7 months — this is a renewal/expansion trigger, not an adoption problem.

[action] Open an expansion conversation immediately citing the Q1 overage pattern ($933 consumed above ACV) and the $13,860 uncovered budget for Jun-Dec. Position a mid-year true-up or capacity increase before June to prevent a service disruption that would damage the adoption momentum already established.

[action] Validate with the Abbott BI team which use cases drove the 112.5% Q1 attainment and confirm whether the Apr-May flattening to exactly $2,486 reflects a usage cap rather than reduced demand. Use this evidence to size the renewal uplift and lock in continued consumption through fiscal year end.

[insight] Five consecutive months of attainment below 72% (Jan 71.0%, Feb 63.2%, Mar 71.5%, Apr 59.1%, May 60.3%) with a YTD consumption of $43,871 against a YTD budget of $67,530 — this is not a ramp issue but a structural under-utilization pattern. SAP HANA Cloud is being treated as a sized-but-idle platform, indicating workloads originally scoped for migration or net-new development haven't landed on the service.

[insight] Cumulative ACV gap over Jan–May totals $44,389 against a full-year ACV of $211,824, meaning roughly 21% of annual contracted value is already unrecoverable unless monthly consumption materially accelerates above the $17,652 ceiling — which the customer has never approached. At the current 5-month run rate of ~$8,775/month, FY2026 will close near $105K consumed, leaving ~$107K (50%) of ACV on the table.

[action] Convene a workload mapping workshop within 30 days to identify 2–3 specific Abbott data workloads (e.g., analytics marts, SAP Datasphere integrations, or app-side persistence) that can be migrated onto HANA Cloud before end of Q2 FY2026 — targeting a step-change from ~$8.3K/month to the $13.8K+ budgeted run rate.

[action] Establish a monthly consumption review with Abbott's HANA Cloud technical owner, anchored on closing the $5,484 May budget gap first, then driving July–Dec attainment above 90% — this protects the remaining $101K of budgeted consumption across the final 7 months.

solutions_l1.name: BAIP - Technology Foundation
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] The Technology Foundation layer at Abbott is operating in an architecturally lopsided state: Integration Suite is functioning as the load-bearing backbone — sustaining 107-112% budget consumption every month and embedded in active production interfaces — while SAP Build, the low-code extensibility layer meant to capitalize on those integrations, is disengaging. Architecturally, Integration Suite exposes APIs and orchestrates data flows precisely so that Build can compose custom apps and workflows on top; Abbott is investing heavily in the pipes but not in the experiences and automations that monetize them, leaving extension and citizen-development capability dormant despite $3,553 in contracted ACV.

[insight] SAP Build's flat $112-$120/month band — a disengagement curve trending to ~41% of FY26 plan — indicates the platform's core capabilities (business app creation, process automation, workflow extension of SAP core) were used for an initial proof point and never scaled to new use cases or teams. This is a classic 'one-app-and-stop' adoption pattern: the low-code factory exists but has no pipeline of business-sponsored workloads feeding it, meaning Abbott is paying for an extensibility platform while continuing to absorb customization demand elsewhere (likely as custom code or shadow IT on top of the very integrations Integration Suite is successfully delivering).

[action] Convene a joint Integration Suite + Build architecture review with Abbott in the next 30 days to map the ~$28K/month of active integration flows to candidate Build use cases — every high-value interface in production is a latent trigger for a workflow, approval app, or process automation, and surfacing 2-3 of these converts dormant Build ACV into business outcomes while reinforcing the Integration Suite expansion narrative.

[action] Initiate an Integration Suite true-up now to lift the ACV ceiling ahead of the projected ~$44K Q4 shortfall, and package it with a Build activation commitment of $315+/month starting June under a named Abbott executive sponsor — bundling the true-up with a Build re-engagement plan protects the renewal of both products and prevents the $2,000+ Build ACV gap from hardening into a contraction at renewal.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption has declined month-over-month from $144 in Jan to $112 in Apr before a marginal rebound to $115 in May — a 20% drop across the first five months. This is not a ramp-up curve; it's a disengagement curve, signaling that initial users built something early and never returned to expand usage to new apps or teams.

[insight] Cumulative consumption through May is $611 against a YTD budget of $1,546 (39.5% attainment), and only $2,527 in ACV has been activated against a full-year budget of $3,553. At the current ~$122/month run rate, Abbott will finish FY26 around $1,465 consumed — roughly 41% of plan — leaving over $2,000 of contracted value unrealized.

[action] Run a use-case discovery workshop in the next 30 days to identify 2-3 net-new Build projects (process automation or business app extensions) that can be activated in Q3 — the flat $112-$120 monthly band proves the current footprint is saturated and growth requires new workloads, not optimization of existing ones.

[action] Establish a monthly consumption milestone of $315+ starting June (matching the forward budget pace) and tie it to a named executive sponsor at Abbott — without a stepped commitment, the remaining 7 months will replicate the Jan-May pattern and the $2,000+ ACV gap will harden into a renewal risk.

solutions_l1.name: Customer Experience
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Abbott's Customer Experience architecture is split-brained: the front-office engagement layer (Cloud for Customer) is underutilized while the back-office monetization layer (SAP Incentive Management) runs at full throttle. SIM is processing ~$52K/month in commission calculations against a sales motion that C4C shows is only being executed by a narrow user base at ~60% of planned consumption — meaning Abbott is paying out incentives on pipeline and deals that are being managed largely outside the contracted CRM system of record, undermining the auditability and selling-behavior feedback loop SIM is architecturally designed to deliver.

[insight] The C4C plateau ($7.2K–$8.6K for five months against a $13.8K budget) is not a consumption problem, it is a capability-activation problem — opportunity management, activity capture, and mobile CRM appear dormant, leaving Cloud for Customer functioning as a contact database rather than the sales execution platform that should be feeding clean deal and quota data downstream into SAP Incentive Management. Without that integration live, SIM's 'improve selling behavior' and 'real-time performance insight' capabilities cannot be realized regardless of how cleanly it calculates payouts.

[action] Run a joint C4C–SIM architecture workshop with Abbott's sales operations and comp admin leads to map which opportunity, quota, and crediting data flows from Cloud for Customer into Incentive Management today; commit to activating at least one additional C4C capability (opportunity or activity management) for one new business unit in the next 60 days so SIM is calculating against CRM-sourced deals, not spreadsheets — recovering ~$19K of the C4C budget gap by year end.

[action] Open a joint ticket with Abbott's SIM admin and SAP support to root-cause the May -$51,754 entry (commission reversal, period reopen, or billing correction) and document it formally before the June QBR so the -98.1% May attainment is not misread as churn; lock the Jan–Apr ~$52,500/month run rate as the baseline and confirm June posts back in range to isolate the event.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption has plateaued in the $7K–$8.6K range for five straight months (Jan $7,245 → May $8,376), while monthly budget sits near $13.8K — this flat curve indicates Cloud for Customer is being used by a fixed, narrow group rather than expanding across sales/service teams. Adoption has stalled at roughly 60% of plan with no upward trajectory.

[insight] ACV stepped up from $19,566 to $23,980 in March, but consumed value actually dipped from $8,633 in April to $8,376 in May — the contract ceiling grew while usage softened, widening the ACV gap to $15,604. With 7 months left and a cumulative budget gap of $28,360 already accrued, the customer is on track to leave significant Cloud for Customer entitlement unused at year end.

[action] Run a Cloud for Customer adoption workshop with Abbott's sales operations lead to identify 2–3 unused core capabilities (e.g., opportunity management, activity capture, mobile CRM) and commit to onboarding one additional business unit or region in the next 60 days — closing even half the $5,456 May budget gap monthly would recover ~$19K by fiscal year end.

[action] Build a 7-month consumption ramp plan targeting the remaining $97,983 of budgeted spend (Jun–Dec), with monthly checkpoints tied to active user growth and transaction volume — escalate to the Abbott executive sponsor if June consumption does not break above the $8.6K April ceiling.

[insight] May posted a negative consumption value of -$51,754, reversing the $53,877 logged in April and dragging cumulative consumed value down sharply — this is a credit, reversal, or data correction event, not normal usage. SAP Incentive Management is a calculation engine for commissions; a reversal of this magnitude typically points to a recalc, retro-adjustment, or billing dispute that warrants immediate investigation before it distorts the adoption narrative for the rest of FY26.

[insight] Jan–Apr ran at 98–102% budget attainment ($51,840 / $52,267 / $52,283 / $53,877 against a $52,747 monthly budget), showing the customer had a steady, healthy consumption rhythm for four consecutive months. The May anomaly breaks an otherwise textbook adoption pattern — meaning the underlying usage is sticky, and recovery is about resolving the May event, not rebuilding adoption from scratch.

[action] Open a joint ticket with Abbott's SAP Incentive Management admin and SAP support to identify the source of the -$51,754 May entry — confirm whether it is a commission reversal, period reopen, or billing correction, and document it so the May attainment of -98.1% does not get misread as a churn signal in QBR reporting.

[action] Lock in the Jan–Apr run rate (~$52,500/month) as the baseline and schedule a consumption review for end of June; if June posts back in the $52K range, the May event is isolated and no intervention is needed beyond monitoring the $3,659 ACV gap from April's overage against the uplifted $57,536 ACV ceiling.

solutions_l1.name: Finance and Spend Management
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Abbott's Finance & Spend Management architecture shows a procurement stack that is contractually complete but operationally narrow — Ariba Sourcing (~87% attainment), Ariba Buying & Invoicing (~56% attainment, capped in a $15–21K band), and Ariba Supplier Risk (21% attainment, flat at ~$714/month) are all deployed but functioning as isolated point tools rather than an integrated source-to-pay process. Sourcing events are running, but their outcomes are not flowing into broader buying volume or supplier risk monitoring, meaning awarded suppliers are not being onboarded, transacted, or continuously risk-assessed at scale. The $120K YTD gap in Buying & Invoicing and the projected 78% shortfall in Supplier Risk are symptoms of the same architectural problem: Abbott is governing a narrow slice of spend end-to-end while the majority leaks to off-platform channels.

[insight] The planning and procurement value chains are both architecturally under-leveraged in opposite ways — SAC Planning (LPR001) is stalled at 53% utilization ($238K consumed vs $337K YTD budget) because it is confined to a single planning domain rather than unifying financial and operational planning as designed, while Preferred Success Ariba is saturated at 100%+ ACV ($138K consumed Jan–May), signaling Abbott is rationing the very advisory capacity that could drive the procurement adoption expansion needed elsewhere. The LoB has bought the capability to plan across workforce, capex, and sales and to accelerate Ariba adoption through Preferred Success, but is using neither at architectural intent — one is under-scoped, the other is ceiling-bound.

[action] Convene a Q2 source-to-pay architecture review with Abbott's procurement leadership that treats Sourcing, Buying & Invoicing, and Supplier Risk as one connected process rather than three contracts: identify 2–3 high-spend categories currently sourced offline, migrate them through Ariba Sourcing, route awarded suppliers into Buying & Invoicing catalogs, and auto-enroll them into Supplier Risk monitoring — this single integrated motion addresses the $120K Buying gap, the $6.9K Sourcing gap, and the $13K Supplier Risk gap simultaneously by lifting transaction volume across the whole stack.

[action] Reallocate the saturated Preferred Success Ariba engagement capacity toward a 60-day SAC Planning use-case expansion (headcount and OpEx planning) and a parallel Ariba adoption sprint, and open a renewal-cycle expansion conversation for Preferred Success now — this converts Abbott's one over-consumed asset into the delivery engine that closes the ~$215K projected SAC shortfall and the ~$183K projected Ariba Buying shortfall before FY26 close.

contract_insights: [insight] Five consecutive months of consumption between $42K-$51K against a monthly budget climbing from $67K to $68K — attainment has never crossed 76% and dipped to 63.1% in May. This is not a ramp problem; it is a stalled adoption plateau where a fixed user base is using SAC Planning for a narrow set of planning cycles rather than expanding into new planning domains (workforce, capex, sales planning).

[insight] Cumulative consumed YTD is $238,344 against a YTD budget of $337,372 — a $99,028 shortfall in just 5 months, while $446,261 of ACV ($89,197 × 5) has already been billed. Abbott is paying for capacity at a 53% utilization rate on contracted ACV, and with 7 months left at the current ~$47K/month run rate, full-year consumption will land near $567K versus $782K budget — a projected 27% shortfall.

[action] Launch a planning use-case expansion workshop within 30 days targeting two net-new planning processes (e.g., headcount or OpEx planning) to lift monthly consumption from the $47K plateau toward the $68K budget line — closing even half the gap recovers ~$75K of consumption over the remaining 7 months.

[action] Investigate the May drop to $42,876 (lowest month YTD) with the Abbott planning lead before June close — identify whether it reflects a completed planning cycle, user attrition, or model decommissioning, and use the finding to build a rolling consumption forecast tied to Abbott's FY26 planning calendar.

[insight] Consumption has held in a narrow $15K–$21K band across all five reported months (Jan $16.9K, Feb $17.9K, Mar $18.5K, Apr $21.3K, May $15.1K) while monthly budgets sat at $27K–$31K — this flat pattern indicates a stable but capped user base on Ariba Buying and Invoicing, not a ramp. Abbott is using the platform for a steady subset of transactions rather than expanding catalog coverage, supplier enablement, or invoice automation breadth.

[insight] May attainment dropped to 55.8% after April's high of 67.8%, and the cumulative ACV gap across Jan–May already totals $120,001 against a remaining annual budget of $238,400 across 7 months — at the current ~$17.9K monthly run rate, Abbott will finish FY2026 near $214K consumed versus ~$397K total budget, roughly 54% attainment. Closing this requires a step-change in transaction volume, not incremental nudges.

[action] Launch a supplier and catalog enablement sprint in June targeting the categories driving off-platform spend at Abbott — every 10 additional suppliers transacting through Ariba should lift monthly consumption by a measurable increment and break the $15K–$21K ceiling visible in the data.

[action] Schedule a Q2 executive business review with Abbott procurement leadership using the $120K Jan–May ACV gap and the projected 54% year-end attainment as the anchor — convert this into a written 7-month adoption plan with monthly consumption targets of ~$34K to recover budget alignment.

[insight] Five consecutive months of consumption below budget (Jan 90.2% → Feb 84.9% → Mar 80.6% → Apr 89.5% → May 88.6%) with a cumulative budget gap of $6,932 — this is not a ramp problem, it's a structural under-utilization pattern. Abbott's sourcing teams are running events but not at the volume Ariba Sourcing was sized for, indicating either a narrow set of categories in scope or sourcing activity leaking to offline/email-based RFx.

[insight] May ACV jumped to $11,102 (from $10,748) while consumption dropped to $8,549 — the contract ceiling expanded but actual usage moved in the opposite direction, widening the ACV gap to $2,554 in a single month. With 7 months remaining and an aggregate forward budget of $65,273, Abbott is on track to leave significant contracted value unconsumed unless event throughput increases materially.

[action] Run a category-coverage workshop with Abbott's procurement leads to identify 2–3 high-spend categories currently sourced outside Ariba (e.g., indirect, MRO, professional services) and migrate them onto the platform in Q2 — this directly addresses the consistent 10–20% monthly shortfall.

[action] Establish a monthly sourcing event target tied to the $9,400+ average budget for Jun–Dec, and instrument a CSM-led adoption sprint (enablement refresh, template library, buyer office hours) to lift attainment from the current ~87% trend toward 100% before fiscal year close.

[insight] Consumption has been flat in a narrow $611–$821 band for five consecutive months (Jan–May) against monthly budgets of ~$3,200–$3,480 — this is not a ramp curve, it's a steady-state plateau indicating Ariba Supplier Risk is being used by a small, fixed group for a narrow use case rather than embedded into broader supplier onboarding and monitoring workflows.

[insight] Cumulative YTD consumption is $3,570 against a YTD budget of $16,777 (21.3% attainment), and with 7 months left at the current ~$714/month run rate Abbott will land at roughly $8,568 consumed versus the full-year budget of ~$39,545 — a projected ~78% shortfall that materially threatens renewal justification unless adoption is restructured now.

[action] Launch a 60-day Supplier Risk activation sprint with Abbott's procurement risk owner: expand monitored supplier population beyond the current narrow set, enable risk alerts/engagement workflows, and target a step-change to at least $2,000/month consumption by July to begin closing the $13,207 YTD budget gap.

[action] Schedule an executive business review within 30 days to present the 21.3% YTD attainment and projected 78% shortfall, and co-build a written consumption recovery plan tying Supplier Risk to Abbott's specific risk domains (financial, regulatory, ESG) so usage is anchored to business outcomes rather than license awareness.

[insight] Consumption has hit 100% of ACV every month from Jan through Mar ($28,355 each) and continues to exceed budget in May at 128% attainment ($27,533 consumed vs $21,505 budgeted) — Abbott is a power user fully saturating their Preferred Success Ariba entitlement, signaling the contract ceiling, not adoption, is now the constraint.

[insight] April broke a three-month streak of 100% ACV consumption, dropping to $26,653 (a $1,701 ACV gap) before rebounding in May — this single-month dip in an otherwise saturated pattern indicates a discrete disruption (resource, ticket backlog, or engagement pause) rather than a trend, and warrants a root-cause review to prevent recurrence.

[action] Initiate an expansion conversation now: with 7 months remaining and Jan–May already consuming $138,851 against the full-year envelope, model an uplift scenario for FY2026 renewal so Abbott isn't rationing high-value Preferred Success engagements in H2.

[action] Run a 30-minute April variance review with the Abbott engagement lead to identify what caused the $1,701 ACV gap, then codify a monthly consumption checkpoint to keep utilization pinned at the 100% ACV ceiling through December.

solutions_l1.name: Supply Chain Management
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Abbott's Supply Chain Management architecture in SAP is currently single-pillar: IBP is the only contracted planning capability, which means demand sensing, S&OP, inventory optimization, and supply response are all expected to flow through one platform. The flat $123,616/month consumption pattern — identical to the dollar across five months — is characteristic of a subscription draw, not a usage curve, so we cannot yet confirm whether Abbott is actually exercising the full IBP capability stack (demand, supply, S&OP, response) or just one module. Architecturally, IBP is designed as a unified collaborative planning process; if only forecasting is live while inventory optimization and response planning remain dormant, Abbott is paying for an integrated platform but running it as a point solution.

[insight] With $618,080 consumed YTD against a $1,483,404 annual contract and a clean glide path to 100% attainment, the financial signal is healthy but architecturally uninformative — ratable burn tells us nothing about planner engagement, planning cycle frequency, or scenario depth. For a planning platform whose value is realized in decisions made (S&OP cycles closed, demand plans published, supply scenarios modeled), dollar attainment is a lagging and misleading KPI. Abbott is at risk of arriving at FY2027 renewal with full consumption but no documented business outcomes, which weakens both expansion potential and renewal defensibility.

[action] Convene a Q2 IBP architecture review with Abbott's supply chain planning leadership to map which IBP modules (demand, inventory, S&OP, response) are actively in production versus dormant, and prioritize activation of at least two underutilized modules — demand sensing and S&OP scenario planning are the highest-leverage candidates — to convert the flat subscription burn into measurable planning capability before H2 FY2026.

[action] Stand up an IBP adoption scorecard that tracks planner logins, planning runs executed, forecast accuracy, and scenarios modeled monthly, and pair it with documented business outcomes (cycle time, forecast bias, inventory turns) to build the evidence base for an FY2027 expansion conversation rather than a flat renewal.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption is tracking at exactly 100% of budget for five consecutive months (Jan–May), with $123,616 consumed against $123,617 budgeted each month — this perfectly linear, ratable burn signals a fixed-allocation or subscription-style draw rather than usage-driven adoption. IBP value realization should be measured by planning cycles run and user engagement, not just dollars consumed, because the current pattern would hold true even if actual platform usage dropped to zero.

[insight] $618,080 has been consumed year-to-date against a full-year budget of $1,483,404, leaving $865,324 (58%) to be realized across the remaining 7 months at the same $123,617/month cadence — the contract is on a clean glide path to 100% attainment, but there is zero buffer or expansion signal, meaning Abbott is consuming exactly what they bought and nothing more. This is the moment to surface incremental IBP use cases (e.g., demand sensing, S&OP scenario planning) before renewal conversations begin.

[action] Schedule a Q2 business review with Abbott's supply chain planning leadership to validate that the steady $123,616/month consumption maps to active IBP planning cycles (demand, supply, S&OP) and identify at least two underutilized modules that could be activated in H2 FY2026.

[action] Build an IBP adoption scorecard that goes beyond the 100% financial attainment — track planner logins, planning runs executed, and scenarios modeled monthly — to give Abbott a true health view and create the evidence base for an expansion conversation ahead of FY2027 renewal.

enterprise_architecture_insights: [insight] Border States has built a federated SAP estate where each L1 domain operates as a vertical silo with no horizontal integration fabric activated — Employee Central is a system of record but doesn't feed Compensation, Commerce Cloud runs without Service/Marketing/CDP, Concur's Expense layer has decoupled from ExpenseIt/Pay/Audit, and BDC powers infrastructure but no Datasphere/SAC/Joule consumption surfaces. The architectural fact is that the customer has bought systems of record across every domain but has not invested in the cross-domain orchestration layer, which blocks the very outcomes (pay-for-performance, unified commerce, closed-loop spend, data-driven decisions) that justify a multi-LoB SAP footprint.

[insight] The Technology Foundation is the only LoB running hot (Joule Studio 209%, Integration Suite 119%) while every consuming domain around it is cold or flat — this inversion is architecturally telling: BTP is being burned by a small set of AI/integration use cases that are NOT wired into HCM, CX, Finance, or Data & AI workloads, meaning Border States is paying twice (once for BTP capacity, once for domain ACV) without the integration chain that would convert BTP spend into LoB outcomes. If Integration Suite were actually carrying the Performance→Compensation, Commerce→Service, or BDC→Datasphere flows, the consuming domains would not be sitting at 0–48% attainment.

[insight] At the enterprise contract level, Border States is operating a structurally unbalanced portfolio — Technology Foundation and Finance/Spend are over-consuming and need true-ups within 30–60 days, while Data & AI (62% unallocated on $215K), Customer Experience ($183K ACV gap on $224K), and HCM talent modules (Compensation at 0%, Career at 42%) leave well over $300K of contracted capacity dormant with only 7 months remaining. The enterprise is simultaneously under-capacity on the platform layer and over-capacity on the outcome layer, which is the signature of a customer that bought an integrated vision but is executing as disconnected point solutions, putting FY2026 renewal narratives at risk across three LoBs at once.

[action] Convene an Enterprise Architecture Review in June with Border States' CIO, CHRO, CFO, and Head of Digital Commerce in one room to map the four broken integration chains (Performance→Compensation→EC Payroll; BDC→Datasphere/SAC; Commerce→Service/Marketing; Concur Expense→Pay/Audit) onto a single Integration Suite-anchored roadmap, converting the existing BTP overconsumption into the connective tissue that activates dormant ACV in Data & AI, CX, and HCM talent.

[action] Run a consolidated FY2026 contract rebalancing motion within 45 days that pairs the Technology Foundation uplift (Joule + Integration Suite) and Concur true-up (~$17K) with explicit activation commitments on the under-consumed LoBs — SF Compensation for the merit cycle, one net-new Commerce Cloud workload, and one Data & AI service on BDC — so commercial expansion on hot LoBs is contractually tied to outcome activation on cold LoBs rather than negotiated as five independent conversations.

[action] Launch a 90-day Clean Core and Integration Coherence workshop anchored on BTP to inventory where Joule agents and iflows are actually landing today vs. where the architectural design intended them to land, then publish a single enterprise integration map that governs BTP as one platform of record across all five L1 domains — this protects the next S/4HANA upgrade path and gives Border States a defensible renewal narrative that the SAP estate is one architecture, not five line items.

solutions_l1.name: BAIP - Data & AI
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] The Data & AI foundation is operationally embedded but architecturally narrow — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (BDC) is the only active product in this LoB, and while it is consuming steadily (93.2% YTD attainment, 5 of 5 months tracking near or above plan), it is functioning as a standalone runtime rather than as the substrate for a broader Data & AI portfolio. Without companion services (e.g., Datasphere, SAC, Joule, AI Foundation) drawing on this platform, the LoB is paying for capacity that is powering infrastructure consumption rather than business-facing data and AI outcomes.

[insight] The annual contract is structurally under-shaped for the LoB's current usage pattern — $81,444 consumed against a $215,855 annual commitment leaves 62% of budget unallocated with only 7 months remaining, requiring a ~18% lift in monthly run-rate ($16.3K → $19.2K) to land the contract. This gap is not a consumption problem but an architectural one: the EA was sized for a wider Data & AI footprint than what is currently deployed, signaling that planned workloads (likely AI/ML, data fabric, or analytics extensions) were never activated on the platform.

[action] Convene a June workload expansion review with the Data & AI LoB owner to map the original EA business case against deployed services, identify the 1–2 dormant Data & AI capabilities (Datasphere, SAC, AI Foundation, or Joule extensions) that were intended to run on this platform, and activate at least one to lift monthly run-rate toward $19.2K and convert infrastructure spend into LoB outcomes.

[action] Document the May 113.7% attainment drivers and codify them as the baseline workload profile, then build a renewal narrative that either right-sizes the EA to actual architectural scope or commits the customer to a defined activation roadmap for the remaining Data & AI stack before FY2026 close.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption is healthy and tracking to plan — 5 months in, the customer has consumed $81,444 against a budget of $87,397 (93.2% attainment), with 3 of 5 months exceeding 100% (Jan 110.6%, Apr 101.7%, May 113.7%). This pattern signals steady, embedded usage of Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement rather than experimental activity, indicating the solution is operationally adopted.

[insight] Despite strong run-rate, the customer is pacing below total annual budget — $81,444 consumed against a full-year budget of $215,855 means $134,411 (62%) remains across 7 months, requiring ~$19,202/month going forward versus the ~$16,289/month average so far. Without expanding workloads or onboarding new use cases, the customer will underconsume by roughly $20K by year-end.

[action] Schedule a workload expansion review in June to identify 1–2 additional services or extensions on Cloud Platform that can lift the monthly run-rate from ~$16.3K to the ~$19.2K needed to fully consume the annual budget.

[action] Lock in May's 113.7% attainment as the new baseline by documenting which workloads drove the uplift, then replicate those drivers across the remaining 7 months to sustain momentum and protect renewal positioning.

solutions_l1.name: BAIP - Technology Foundation
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] The BTP foundation underpinning Border States' extensibility and integration strategy is operating beyond its contracted envelope across two distinct capability layers — Joule Studio (AI/agent development on BTP) is running at 209% of plan and Integration Suite (the iPaaS backbone for S/4 and surrounding systems) is at 119% YTD. Architecturally this signals that the Technology Foundation LoB has moved past pilot into embedded production use: Joule-based AI extensions and Integration Suite flows are now load-bearing for business processes, not experimental. The risk is not adoption — it is that the commercial envelope was sized for a foundation, while the customer is operating it as a platform of record.

[insight] The two products are functionally coupled and the consumption pattern reflects that coupling — Joule Studio agents and extensions almost always invoke Integration Suite endpoints to reach SAP and non-SAP systems of record, so Joule's 2x burn is very likely a leading indicator of further Integration Suite pressure in H2. With Joule on pace to exhaust its $24,551 annual budget by June and Integration Suite trending to ~$113K against a $77K plan, the LoB is approaching a compound capacity event where a hard stop on either product would stall the AI and integration roadmap simultaneously, not independently.

[action] Convene a single joint consumption and architecture review within 2 weeks covering both Joule Studio and Integration Suite together — map which Joule agents/extensions are driving Integration Suite calls, classify workloads as strategic production vs. unoptimized/non-prod, and produce one consolidated 7-month forecast so the BTP foundation is governed as one platform rather than two line items.

[action] Initiate a CPEA true-forward / capacity uplift conversation now, before the Joule budget exhausts in June, and structure it as a Technology Foundation expansion (Joule + Integration Suite) tied to the AI and integration roadmap; pair the commercial uplift with governance guardrails — tagging, environment cleanup, quotas on non-production Joule workspaces, and iflow consolidation — so the expanded envelope funds strategic growth rather than absorbing drift.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption is running at roughly 2x budget every single reported month — Jan through May posted $4,829, $3,852, $4,519, $4,245, and $3,795 against monthly budgets near $2,025, averaging 209.8% attainment. This is not a one-time spike; it's a sustained over-consumption pattern indicating the platform is embedded in active workloads that are scaling beyond plan.

[action] Schedule a consumption review with the customer within the next 2 weeks to validate workload drivers behind the 209% average attainment, identify which services or environments are pulling spend, and confirm whether current usage reflects intended production scale or unoptimized configurations.

[action] Initiate a true-forward or capacity expansion conversation now — with the FY2026 budget on track to exhaust by June, proactively model a contract uplift or additional capacity block before the customer hits the ceiling and faces a reactive commercial escalation.

[insight] Consumption has exceeded budget in 4 of 5 reported months (Jan 131%, Feb 115%, Apr 130%, May 123%), with cumulative consumed of $36,864 against a budget of $31,606 — a $5,258 overrun in just 5 months. This is a sustained over-consumption pattern, not a one-time spike, signaling that real workloads are running on the platform and adoption is healthy and accelerating.

[insight] At the current 5-month run rate of $7,373/month, projected full-year consumption is roughly $88,476 against a remaining annual budget of $77,140 ($76,745 budgeted for Jun-Dec + $36,864 already consumed = $113,609 trajectory vs. $77,140 total annual plan) — the customer is on pace to burn through the annual ceiling before fiscal year end, creating a mid-year capacity risk.

[action] Schedule a consumption review with Border States within 30 days to validate the workloads driving the overrun, confirm they are strategic (not runaway/test), and begin a true-up or expansion conversation before Q3 to avoid a hard stop on production usage.

solutions_l1.name: Customer Experience
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Customer Experience for Border States is architecturally single-threaded on SAP Commerce Cloud — the platform is the designated system of engagement for B2B catalog management, pricing, promotions, and unified storefront experiences, but the flat $3.4K/month consumption signature indicates only one storefront or business unit is actually transacting. The 'grow sales across every channel' and 'seamless unified commerce' capabilities are contractually licensed but functionally dormant, meaning Border States is paying for a multi-channel commerce backbone while operating it as a single-catalog web shop.

[insight] The Customer Experience LoB has no surrounding CX architecture activated — Commerce Cloud is running in isolation without adjacent capabilities like Service, Marketing, or Customer Data that would normally drive traffic, personalization, and repeat buyer behavior into the storefront. This explains the locked consumption band: with $17.3K YTD against a $54.1K YTD budget (32% attainment) and a projected $41.5K annual landing against a $224.7K ACV ceiling, the storefront has no demand-generation or post-sale engagement engine feeding it, so consumption cannot expand organically.

[action] Run a Commerce Cloud workload expansion workshop in the next 30 days to activate a second revenue stream on the existing platform — a second B2B catalog, punchout integration for electrical distribution buyers, or a new customer segment — since the flat consumption pattern will not break without a net-new workload, and one comparable workload would roughly double monthly run-rate and lift attainment toward 65%.

[action] Build a 7-month catch-up plan targeting ~$15K/month consumption anchored to Border States revenue milestones (peak ordering season, distributor onboarding waves) rather than generic feature rollout, so adoption is tied to measurable commerce events that close the $76K ACV gap before fiscal year end.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption is locked in a tight $3,378–$3,554 band for five straight months (Jan–May) against a monthly budget climbing from $10,750 to $10,921 — this is a textbook flat-adoption signature for SAP Commerce Cloud, indicating the storefront is live but only a single workload or business unit is actually transacting on it. The platform is not expanding into additional catalogs, channels, or buyer segments.

[insight] Cumulative consumption is $17,317 against a YTD budget of $54,108 (32% attainment) with $76,213 of ACV gap accrued in just 5 months. Projecting the current ~$3,463/month run rate across the remaining 7 months yields roughly $41,558 total annual consumption — about 37% of the $224,712 annual ACV ceiling. enterprise_architecture_insights: [insight] Domino's has contracted an integrated SAP enterprise stack — BTP as the extensibility and integration backbone, HANA Cloud as the data foundation, IBP + Business Network as the demand-to-supply loop, Ariba S2C+P2P as the procurement spine, and Concur for T&E — but the architecture is splintering into two halves: the *transactional and integration plumbing* layers (Integration Suite at 99%, Ariba Buying at 99.96%, Concur stack at 108–123%) are healthy or over-running, while every *strategic, planning, and value-creation* layer above them (SAP Build 52.7%, HANA Cloud 75.2%, Ariba Sourcing/Contracts 66%, IBP and Business Network flat-lined) is disengaging. The big-picture consequence: Domino's is paying ~$1M+ in FY2026 contracts to run the business but is no longer using SAP to *improve* the business — pizza is moving, invoices are paying, APIs are flowing, but sourcing rigor, supply chain resilience, AI-driven insight, and net-new digital experiences are all stalled, which is exactly the capability set a QSR leader needs to defend margin against commodity inflation and franchisee pressure in FY2026.

[insight] The architectural pattern across three independent L1 domains points to one shared root cause — a missing 'data + extensibility' activation layer. HANA Cloud is stagnating because Datasphere/SAC aren't pulling on it; SAP Build is collapsing because no new apps are being authored on top of Integration Suite; IBP and Business Network are flat because supplier-side data isn't being wired into the planning brain. All three are symptoms of the same gap: Domino's has no active program turning contracted SAP capability into net-new data products, workflows, or supplier integrations. Without this activation layer, every other domain will continue to consume only its transactional floor, and Domino's BTP-anchored clean-core ambition (extensions on the platform, not in S/4) cannot materialize — meaning customizations will inevitably leak back into the core and create upgrade debt.

[insight] Domino's FY2026 contract position is structurally mis-shaped against actual demand, and the mis-shaping is bidirectional within the same enterprise: Concur T&E will breach ACV ceilings between August and October (service-disruption risk on a working capability), while BTP Build, Custom Domain, HANA Cloud, Ariba Sourcing/Contracts, IBP, and Business Network will collectively leave an estimated $200K+ unrealized by year-end (renewal-risk on dormant capabilities). The enterprise is simultaneously over-consuming what works and under-consuming what was bought to transform — a clear signal that FY2026 was contracted to an architectural ambition the customer has not operationalized, and FY2027 renewal conversations will be fragmented and defensive unless the portfolio is rebaselined as one coherent commercial decision.

[action] Convene a single Enterprise Architecture Review with Domino's CIO, CDO, and LoB owners (Procurement, Supply Chain, Finance, Digital) within 45 days, framed around one question: 'Which 5 cross-domain business outcomes will Domino's commit to delivering on the SAP stack in H2 FY2026?' — for example, supplier-collaboration-to-IBP-to-HANA closed-loop resiliency, Sourcing-to-Contracts-to-Buying compliance restoration, and Build+Integration Suite+Custom Domain franchisee/loyalty app launches. This replaces four disconnected product conversations with one architectural commitment and creates pull-through demand across the dormant layers simultaneously.

[action] Stand up a 'BTP Activation Squad' (joint SAP + Domino's, 90-day mandate) whose sole charter is to land 3–5 net-new workloads that consume the underutilized platform layer end-to-end: each workload must touch HANA Cloud (data), Datasphere/SAC (consumption), SAP Build (app/workflow), Integration Suite (already healthy), and Custom Domain (branded surface). Candidate workloads — franchisee operations portal, supplier onboarding with Business Network handoff, store-level demand sensing into IBP — are deliberately chosen to also pull the stalled Supply Chain and Data & AI domains forward, fixing four L1 gaps with one coordinated motion.

[action] Initiate a consolidated FY2026 contract rebaselining exercise with Domino's CFO and procurement, presenting the entire SAP portfolio as one position: true-up Concur T&E (breach risk by Q3), reconcile Ariba Buying's $68K back-half headroom, and put BTP, HANA Cloud, IBP, and Business Network under-consumption on the table as capability-activation commitments rather than passive shelfware. The goal is to convert FY2027 from four fragmented renewal defenses into one enterprise renewal anchored to the activation roadmap from the EA Review, protecting ~$200K of at-risk ACV and creating a credible expansion path on the over-running domains.

solution_architecture_insights: [insight] The Data & AI foundation for Domino's is architecturally narrow — SAP HANA Cloud is the sole active component, with no evidence of the surrounding Business Data Cloud fabric (SAP Datasphere for data product modeling, SAP Analytics Cloud for consumption, or agentic AI anchoring) being engaged. This means HANA Cloud is being used as a standalone database rather than as the compute backbone of a business data fabric, which explains why workloads are stagnating: without Datasphere-driven data products and SAC-driven analytical consumption pulling on it, there is no demand engine to scale HANA Cloud usage.

At 75.2% YTD attainment and falling, this is not a ramp-up lag; it is a signal that no new analytical or AI workloads are being onboarded onto the platform, and the contracted capacity for mission-critical data and AI use cases is sitting idle.

[action] Run a Business Data Cloud architecture workshop within 30 days to position HANA Cloud alongside Datasphere and SAC, and identify 2–3 concrete data product or AI use cases (e.g., store-level demand signals, delivery analytics, agentic AI on operational data) that will drive HANA Cloud consumption from $7.7K back to the $11K monthly budget line starting June.

Together this signals that net-new BTP workloads and customer-facing app surfaces are not being launched — without new Build apps to deploy and brand, both the domain service and the CPEA runtime have nothing to grow into, putting the entire Technology Foundation at renewal risk despite a healthy integration layer.

Meanwhile Ariba Buying and Invoicing remains on-plan YTD (99.96%), meaning Domino's is still transacting POs and invoices but increasingly without upstream sourcing rigor or contract governance — the P2P tail is executing against agreements that aren't being refreshed in the system of record, putting compliance, savings capture, and supplier accountability at risk. Combined ~$43K of projected unrealized value across Sourcing and Contracts signals the strategic procurement layer is being bypassed while the transactional layer carries the illusion of health.

[insight] The Travel & Expense stack is the inverse problem — architecturally it is firing on all cylinders and structurally under-contracted. Concur Expense (123% YTD), ExpenseIt (108%), Analytics (118%), and Preferred Success (114%) are all over-consuming in a mutually reinforcing pattern: ExpenseIt's mobile OCR is driving more receipts into Expense, which generates more spend data into Analytics, which is consuming advisory hours via Preferred Success to operationalize insights. This is exactly the integrated T&E value loop the architecture is designed to produce. However, every component will breach its ACV ceiling before Q4 (Expense ~August, ExpenseIt pre-Q3, Preferred Success ~October), meaning the LoB faces a service-disruption cliff precisely because adoption succeeded — the FY2026 contract was sized to a demand curve that no longer reflects reality.

Identify 2–3 upcoming categories — packaging, ingredients, logistics — to route through Ariba Sourcing in the next 60 days with a mandate that awards flow into Ariba Contracts authoring, re-establishing the architectural handoff. Pair this with a category-manager enablement refresh on event templates and contract workflows to reverse the disengagement pattern before it normalizes across Q3.

Simultaneously reconcile Ariba Buying's $68K remaining ACV against the $350K back-half budget so the P2P contract position is rebaselined alongside T&E, giving Domino's one coherent FY2026 Finance & Spend coverage decision.
