solutions_l1.name: Supply Chain Management
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Domino's supply chain planning architecture is structurally incomplete — IBP is provisioned as the planning brain for demand sensing, S&OP, and supply response, while Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration is the connective tissue that should feed supplier forecasts, PO commitments, and ASN signals back into IBP. Both products show identical flat-line consumption fingerprints ($9,308 and $55,968 monthly with zero variance for five months), meaning neither the planning engine nor the collaboration network is scaling. The LoB has bought the integrated demand-to-supply loop but is operating it as two dormant subscriptions rather than a closed-loop process.

[insight] The Business Network is functioning as a billing entity rather than a trading partner ecosystem — its architectural value (forecast collaboration, PO collaboration, ASN visibility, procurement resiliency) compounds with supplier onboarding and transaction volume, yet the flat $55,968/month against a budget curve ramping to $74,426 by December reveals a $23K YTD gap widening to $75K+ by year-end. For a business like Domino's with thousands of ingredient, packaging, and equipment suppliers, a static network means supply chain resiliency capabilities are paid for but not protecting the business from disruption.

[action] Launch a joint IBP–Business Network activation initiative that wires supplier forecast commits and PO collaboration flows from the Network directly into IBP's supply response and S&OP planning areas — this closes the architectural loop both products were designed to deliver and creates measurable transaction volume on the Network while expanding IBP scenario usage beyond the current flat baseline.

[action] Convene an executive business review with Domino's supply chain leadership covering both products together, presenting a unified adoption scorecard (active planners, scenarios run, connected suppliers, PO/ASN transaction volume) tied to the combined $186K annual investment, and co-build a Q3–Q4 FY2026 roadmap to onboard top non-digitized suppliers and activate 1–2 net-new IBP use cases such as demand sensing or inventory optimization.

The enterprise is simultaneously buying the agent surface, the process intelligence surface, and the analytics surface while starving the governed API substrate underneath, which means every new AI agent, process model, or planning workload at Lilly is on a trajectory to either bypass governance via point-to-point integrations or stall waiting for trusted APIs — a clean-core architecture is being eroded in real time.

[insight] Across every L1 domain Lilly has fully consumed the Preferred Success/enablement wrapper (PS Ariba locked at $84.8K, PS Commerce at 100% ACV, PS Signavio at 113%, PS SFSF burning ratably) while the underlying platforms those services are meant to accelerate are materially under-adopted (HXM Operations at 38%, Commerce Cloud at 45%, Signavio PI at 19%, SAC Planning at 4.5%, DRC at 0.17%, Batch Release Hub at 0.08%, IBP modules dormant). The success motion is architecturally disconnected from the platform consumption it should be driving — Lilly is paying full price for guided adoption that is not landing as production workloads, which means the FY27 renewal conversation will be defending shelfware across six domains unless the success entitlements are re-pointed at named platform gaps now.

[insight] Lilly's pharma-critical GxP and regulatory architecture has the compliance-adjacent layers live (SF Validated Learning at 96%, SSO trending to overage, Ariba P2P at ceiling) but the regulated execution and reporting layers that close the GxP loop are dark — Batch Release Hub is at $0 with $32K/month ACV burning, Document & Reporting Compliance is at 0.17% across 90+ countries, IBP Supply/Inventory/Response modules are dormant, and SAC Planning has never industrialised. For a pharma manufacturer, this means the digital thread from demand signal → supply plan → batch release → statutory reporting is broken at three of four nodes, leaving Lilly exposed on the exact controls (electronic batch disposition, e-invoicing/VAT, integrated S&OP) that regulators and finance auditors scrutinise — and leaving roughly $300K+ of annualised ACV commercially undefended at renewal.

[action] Convene a single enterprise architecture review in the next 30 days with Lilly's CIO, Integration CoE, and platform owners to formally rebalance the BTP envelope: redirect dormant Integration Suite credits and BDC Public capacity (~$255K+ projected unconsumed) onto a named 7-month pipeline of AI-consumable APIs that explicitly underpin the Joule Studio overrun, the Signavio process threads, the Ariba P2P scale, and the IBP-to-Make/Deliver integration — turning three separate overage/shortfall conversations into one governed clean-core integration roadmap before Q3 contract motions begin.

[action] Launch an enterprise-wide 'Preferred Success re-pointing' program in June that inventories every fully-consumed success entitlement (Ariba, Commerce, Signavio, SFSF, BTP) and contractually ties each remaining hour to a named under-adopted platform workload — HXM Operations process areas, a second Commerce storefront, a Signavio PI→PM closed-loop thread, SAC Planning go-live, and DRC statutory scenario — so the ~$200K+ of success spend in H2 produces documented platform consumption lift rather than generic advisory, and the FY27 renewal is sized on outcomes not entitlements.

The success motion is outpacing the platform consumption it is meant to drive, indicating Preferred Success hours are likely being spent on foundational enablement and use-case scoping rather than scaling live process transformation work.

[action] Re-architect the engagement around a single end-to-end process thread (e.g., Order-to-Cash or GxP-regulated Procure-to-Pay) where Process Intelligence mines the as-is, Process Management documents and governs the to-be, and Preferred Success hours are explicitly tied to that thread — target $6K+/month PI and $4.5K+/month PM consumption through Q3 to close the combined ~$70K YTD gap and prove the closed-loop value before renewal.

solutions_l1.name: Supply Chain Management
solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Kraft Heinz has deployed SAP Business Network Supply Chain Collaboration as the connective tissue between their S/4HANA and IBP environments and their supplier base, but the bursty consumption pattern (March $75K → April $143K → May $125K) reveals the platform is being used as a project-based onboarding tool rather than the steady-state collaboration backbone it was architected to be. Forecast collaboration, PO collaboration, and inventory visibility are designed to run as continuous signal exchanges with suppliers — the volatility indicates that only a subset of document flows are live and supplier activation is happening in episodic waves, leaving the resiliency and transparency capabilities structurally underutilized.

[insight] The YTD trajectory of $456K against a $3.15M annual ceiling — projecting to ~35% full-year attainment — tells us the architectural promise of the Business Network (value scaling with transaction volume across the supplier base) is not being realized because Kraft Heinz is likely running a narrow set of transaction types with a narrow slice of suppliers. For a CPG manufacturer of this scale, this consumption profile is inconsistent with end-to-end procurement and forecast collaboration being operational; it suggests the network is functioning as a PO transmission channel rather than a bidirectional demand-supply synchronization layer feeding IBP.

[action] Commission a transaction-type audit with Kraft Heinz supply chain and procurement leads to map which Business Network document flows (POs, order confirmations, ASNs, invoices, forecast commits, quality notifications) are actually live versus contracted-but-dormant, then sequence activation of 2-3 dormant flows per quarter to convert the contract into continuous collaboration and close the projected $2M attainment gap.

[action] Replicate the April supplier onboarding wave as a repeatable monthly cadence tied to IBP forecast cycles — institutionalizing 1-2 supplier enablement waves per month would both stabilize consumption above the $130K monthly threshold needed to reach 90%+ attainment and operationalize the forecast collaboration capability that justifies the Business Network investment in the first place.

contract_insights: [insight] Consumption jumped from $75K in March to $143K in April (a 91% month-over-month increase pushing attainment to 84.4%), then fell back to $125K in May — this volatility indicates Kraft Heinz is onboarding suppliers or transactions in bursts rather than running steady-state supply chain collaboration. Sustaining the April pace would close the gap; the May dip signals the momentum is fragile.

[insight] Year-to-date consumption totals $456,550 against an ACV of $262,899 monthly ($3.15M annual ceiling) — with 7 months remaining and an average monthly run rate of $91,310, projected full-year consumption lands around $1.10M, roughly 35% of total contract value. This is a significant under-utilization trajectory for a Business Network supply chain product where value scales with supplier transaction volume.

[action] Lock in the April consumption pattern as the new baseline by identifying what drove the spike (likely supplier onboarding wave or new document type activation) and replicate it monthly — a structured supplier enablement cadence of 1-2 waves per month for the remaining 7 months would push attainment from the current 71.7% toward sustained 90%+.

[action] Launch a joint adoption sprint with Kraft Heinz procurement and supply chain leads targeting expansion of transaction types (POs, invoices, ASNs, quality notifications) across their existing supplier base — closing the $137,917 May ACV gap requires roughly $20K/month in incremental consumption, achievable by activating 2-3 dormant document flows already covered under the contract.

enterprise_architecture_insights: [insight] Marathon has contracted a coherent SAP platform spanning S/4HANA Private Cloud, Ariba P2P, Signavio, SAC Planning, and Joule — but the BTP integration and data fabric backbone meant to bind them is architecturally dormant: BDC Public sits at zero, the BTP EA Integration Suite line went dormant after a single January spike, the Finance BTP contract has zero consumption, and the standalone Integration Suite is being throttled down. The enterprise is operating as five disconnected product islands rather than the closed-loop digital core it was bought as, which directly blocks Marathon's refining-sector imperative of margin optimization through real-time spend, operations, and financial signal integration.

[insight] Across every L1 domain the same anti-pattern repeats — the transactional/runtime layer is over-consuming while the governance, design, and intelligence layer is starving: Database Cloud at 537% vs BDC at 0%, Joule Studio at 320% vs Integration Suite dormant, Ariba Buying at 138% vs Catalog at 35%, and S/4HANA Private Cloud running without Preferred Success. Marathon is industrializing execution faster than it is industrializing control, meaning every domain is accruing compliance, architectural drift, and off-contract spend risk simultaneously — a systemic posture issue, not four isolated consumption problems.

[insight] The commercial envelope is materially mis-sized against the architecture in flight — Database Cloud projects ~$108K overage, Ariba Buying ~$155K overage, and Joule Studio is running 3.2x plan, while BDC Public, BTP Foundation Other, Finance BTP, Ariba Catalog, and Preferred Success will collectively strand 35–100% of their entitlements. Marathon will exit FY2026 having overpaid for runtime and underutilized the very capabilities (data products, integration, catalog compliance, success advisory) that convert SAP from infrastructure into business outcomes, undermining the FY2027 renewal narrative.

[action] Within 30 days, convene a Chief Architect-level Enterprise Integration & Data Fabric review with Marathon that treats BTP, Integration Suite, and BDC as one cross-domain backbone — map each over-consuming workload (Joule agents, Ariba Buying, Database Cloud, Signavio Process Intelligence findings, SAC Planning models) to the specific APIs, events, and governed data products it requires, and commit a sequenced Jun–Sep activation plan with named business owners so the dormant platform layer comes online in step with the workloads already in production.

[action] Run a single consolidated FY2026 commercial true-up and FY2027 baseline reset in the next 45 days that addresses Database Cloud, Ariba Buying & Invoicing, and Joule Studio overages as one package, funded in part by repurposing the structurally underutilized lines (BDC Public, BTP Other, Finance BTP, Ariba Catalog headroom, Preferred Success) — preventing mid-year service constraints while re-anchoring the contract shape to the customer's true consumption architecture.

[action] Launch a 'governance and control layer' rescue program within 14 days covering the three starved control points — Ariba Catalog re-enablement, Preferred Success sponsor re-alignment for S/4HANA Private Cloud, and Signavio Process Management CoE cadence linked to Process Intelligence findings — packaged as one executive conversation with Marathon's CFO/COO sponsors, since each gap individually erodes margin discipline but together they signal the enterprise is running execution platforms without their governance scaffolding.

enterprise_architecture_insights: [insight] Stepan's entire SAP architecture is suffering from a single root-cause pattern: the platform substrate is dormant while the application layers above it erode. BTP Integration Suite is down 17% Jan→May and Joule Studio is flat-lining at ~$580/month, while SAP Business Data Cloud and CPEA sit at $0 consumed — meaning the integration fabric and AI/data foundation that should connect HCM's Hire-to-Retire chain, Procurement's Source-to-Pay chain, and any cross-domain analytics simply do not exist in an active state. This is why every downstream domain shows the same signature — disconnected islands of consumption rather than end-to-end process flows — and it forecloses Stepan's ability to deliver the integrated specialty-chemicals operating model (connected procurement, workforce, and operational analytics) that justifies the SAP investment in the first place.

[insight] The integration breakage is visible identically in two independent domains, confirming it is architectural rather than domain-specific: in HCM, Recruiting ($3,938/mo flat), Onboarding ($348/mo declining), EC (91.8% and sliding), and EC Payroll (narrow $4,759–$4,906 band) operate as four disconnected islands instead of one Hire-to-Retire chain; in Procurement, Sourcing (13.9%), Catalog (0.5%), Buying & Invoicing (63.7%), and Concur Expense (82% May collapse) show the same three-of-four-links-broken pattern across Source-to-Pay. Without Integration Suite actively wiring these chains together and without BDC providing a semantic layer for cross-process visibility, Stepan cannot close the loops that drive compliance, cost control, or workforce productivity — the contracted business outcomes are architecturally unreachable regardless of how much adoption work is done inside each module.

[insight] Across all four L1 areas Stepan has consistently purchased the full architectural stack but is consuming only the peripheral, low-risk layers — Concur's analytics/reporting modules run >110% while the transactional Expense core collapses; HCM's Preferred Success entitlement straight-lines $57,705 unused while the talent modules decline; BDC is contracted at $4,807 annual with $0 consumed; Catalog sits at 0.5% on $20,071 YTD. The enterprise pattern is 'shelfware at the foundation, thriving at the edges,' which means Stepan is paying for a strategic SAP platform but operating it as a fragmented set of point tools — a posture that will not survive the FY27 renewal cycle when cumulative non-realization becomes visible to procurement leadership.

[action] Convene a single Enterprise Architecture Review in June that treats BTP Integration Suite + BDC + CPEA as the shared substrate for both the Hire-to-Retire and Source-to-Pay process chains, not as three independent BAIP contracts — the agenda must produce one prioritized integration backlog (Recruiting→Onboarding→EC→Payroll, and Sourcing→Catalog→Buying→Expense) with named platform-owner accountability, sequencing CPEA/BDC provisioning in June-July so the foundation is live before the December activation window and before Q3 talent and procurement cycles begin.

[action] Redirect the two large dormant entitlements — HCM Preferred Success ($57,705 unused over 7 months) and Ariba Preferred Success ($9,765/month passive) — into a single Joule-anchored use case workshop within 45 days that forces consumption across the dormant BTP layers (Joule Studio, Integration Suite) while simultaneously unblocking the declining talent and procurement modules; this converts straight-lined CSM spend into the integration and AI authoring work that the architectural diagnosis proves is the actual gap.

[action] Establish a monthly Cross-Domain Consumption & Integration checkpoint (CEA-led, spanning all four L1 areas) with explicit run-rate gates — ~$277/$284 on CPEA lines, ~$1,500/mo on Joule Studio, ~$4,250/mo on Integration Suite, Catalog above 25% by Q3, Concur Expense submitter-base restored — so that platform activation, integration recovery, and application adoption are tracked as one architectural program rather than four separate LoB scorecards, protecting renewal credibility ahead of FY27.

solution_architecture_insights: [insight] Stepan's HCM foundation is eroding at the core: SF Employee Central — the system of record that feeds payroll, talent, learning, and onboarding — has slid from 95.3% to 91.8% attainment over five months. When the core HRIS is being bypassed for legacy processes, every downstream module inherits the gap, which is exactly what the data shows: Onboarding (41% attainment), Career & Talent Development (51%), and Performance & Goals (76%) are all declining in lockstep. This is not eight independent adoption problems — it is one architectural problem where EC's authoritative employee, org, and position data isn't anchoring the talent suite, so managers and employees revert to offline workflows across the lifecycle.

[insight] The Hire-to-Retire process chain is contracted but operationally fragmented: SF Recruiting is flat-lined at ~$3,938/month (single static usage pattern), SF Onboarding is declining to $348/month (41% attainment), and EC Payroll is locked in a $4,759–$4,906 band indicating a fixed employee subset is being processed while in-scope populations run outside the system. The architecture is designed for Recruiting → Onboarding → EC → Payroll to operate as one continuous flow, but the consumption signatures show three disconnected islands — candidates are being hired without flowing into Onboarding, new hires aren't landing in EC, and EC employees aren't all reaching Payroll. Meanwhile $57,705 of remaining Preferred Success entitlement is straight-lining unused, forfeiting the very CSM and expert capacity that should be unblocking these integration gaps.

[action] Convene a single Hire-to-Retire architecture review with Stepan's HR/HRIS lead in June that treats Recruiting, Onboarding, EC, and EC Payroll as one process chain rather than four products — specifically audit how many Jan–May hires flowed end-to-end through all four systems versus dropped into manual handoffs, and identify which legal entities or employee populations are contracted for EC Payroll but still processed externally. This addresses the flat-line signatures in Recruiting and Payroll and the decline in Onboarding with one diagnosis.

[action] Redirect the unused Preferred Success entitlement ($57,705 over 7 months) into a calendared talent-cycle activation plan that anchors SF Performance & Goals, Compensation, and Career & Talent Development to Stepan's FY2026 HR operating rhythm (mid-year reviews, merit planning, development planning windows). Book specific expert sessions and adoption clinics against each cycle milestone now — this converts straight-lined CSM spend into the recurring process cadence that the declining talent-module consumption proves is currently missing.
